instruction: Recreate this reference line drawing in Illustrator as a vector. No image trace — draw it line by line.
approach: Do not vectorize pixels. Read the reference, model its geometry parametrically (projection axes, profile curves, lattice rules), emit ExtendScript that draws every line, inject over COM, then render-compare and refine.

method.transport: COM app.DoJavaScript on the already-running Illustrator

verification: Export PNG24 via doc.exportFile and visually compare silhouette + construction lines against the reference.
lesson: The agent is the geometry engine; Illustrator is the renderer. Every coordinate is computed, so the result is deterministic and editable.

instruction: Make the JSX robust to Illustrator version and artboard placement.
approach: Work in image space (x right, y DOWN) for intuitive layout, then convert per point to Illustrator coordinates using the active artboardRect.

method.draw_api: ai_x = abLeft + x; ai_y = abTop - y, where [abLeft,abTop]=artboardRect[0..1]

key_parameters.artboardRect: [left, top, right, bottom], Y up, top>bottom

verification: Geometry lands correctly regardless of where the artboard sits in the document.
lesson: Anchor all math to artboardRect instead of assuming origin at 0,0; convert y once at draw time.

instruction: Draw an Eiffel Tower one line at a time — silhouette, iron lattice, platforms, antenna.
approach: Define a half-width(t) profile sampled along height fraction t to get the curved silhouette; draw left/right outer edges as polylines; fill each height band with X cross-braces whose column count scales with local width; carve the base arch with a half-ellipse opening; add 3 platform decks and a tapering antenna.

method.draw_api: pathItems.setEntirePath; loops emit one path per brace

verification: ~172 stroked paths; PNG preview reads as a recognizable Eiffel Tower with open arch.
lesson: Rich line art comes from a small parametric core (profile function + banded lattice loops), not from hand-placing every line.

instruction: Rebuild a perspective staircase: ascending flight, ~15 steps, two wall panels at the top.
approach: Define three projected basis vectors R (run), H (rise), W (width). project(i,j,k)=O+i*R+j*W+k*H. Build near (j=0) and far (j=1) zigzag profiles by alternating riser then tread; draw both polylines and a W-rung connecting every profile vertex; add landing, panels, and enclosing wall diagonals.

method.draw_api: two profile polylines + one rung per vertex

verification: Recognizable flight, but treads looked like thin slats (see next record).
lesson: A clean three-axis projection reproduces a staircase, but the axis proportions decide whether it reads correctly.

instruction: The steps collapsed into a dense venetian-blind hatch — fix so each tread reads as an open step.
approach: Diagnosis: the run vector projected almost flat (tread depth ~8px in screen-Y), so consecutive nosing lines stacked and white tread tops vanished. Fix: give the tread real vertical extent and balance it against the riser.
verification: PNG preview now shows distinct treads with visible white tops.
lesson: When a stepped surface turns into a hatch, the projected tread depth is too small relative to the riser — increase the tread's vertical component.

instruction: The perspective is still wrong — the steps look sheared. Match the reference's true side profile.
approach: Switch to a proper oblique projection: keep the TRUE side profile by making the tread strictly HORIZONTAL (Ry=0) and the riser strictly VERTICAL (Hx=0); push only the stair WIDTH obliquely up-right via W. Earlier tilt on the tread was shearing each step into a parallelogram slat.
verification: Each step now reads as horizontal tread + vertical riser + up-right depth, matching the reference.
lesson: For oblique/cabinet projection of stairs, the silhouette plane (tread horizontal, riser vertical) must stay undistorted; only the receding depth axis is skewed.

instruction: The middle diagonal doesn't line up with the reference — fix it.
constraints: construction lines must connect to real corners
approach: Identify what the line IS: the near wall's TOP edge. It must run PARALLEL to the outer (far-wall) top diagonal and connect two real structural corners — the landing front corner (top) and the bottom-left floor corner (where the far-wall vertical and floor edge also meet). The earlier version anchored its top too high (near panel tops) and its bottom floated, so it crossed the steps at the wrong angle.
verification: The two long diagonals now form a parallel 'wall band' and the middle line meets the corner like the reference.
lesson: A construction line that 'doesn't line up' is usually mislabeled — name the feature, then anchor both endpoints to actual geometry corners instead of eyeballed points.

instruction: How to converge on a faithful match.
approach: Treat it as a control loop: emit JSX -> export PNG24 at reduced scale -> read the image -> diff against the reference (silhouette, slope, key construction lines, proportions) -> adjust a few parameters -> re-run. Close stale documents between runs to avoid clutter; fit the view so the artboard is reviewable.
verification: Each iteration is judged on the exported preview, not assumed correct.
lesson: Always export and look. A few targeted parameter changes per loop beats large rewrites; verify visually every pass.
